name: french lentil salad
instructions:
Two paths for building this: (A) HOT-PAN VERSION — sauté leeks in olive oil until softened (see notes on when to reach for this) or (B) NO-PAN VERSION — skip the leeks entirely and just macerate raw garlic in the vinaigrette (see notes on why this is often the right call).

MAKE THE CREAM VINAIGRETTE (with mustard): whisk heavy cream, white wine vinegar, dijon mustard, and raw minced garlic together. Both the vinegar and the mustard go in — mustard adds savory depth and helps emulsify, vinegar carries the sharp french-vinaigrette acid. Neither alone is enough; the dish needs both. If going the no-pan route, use more garlic here and let it macerate in the vinaigrette while you assemble the rest.

(HOT-PAN VERSION ONLY) Sauté leeks in olive oil over medium heat until softened. Add garlic during the sauté if you're not putting it all in the vinaigrette. Optionally deglaze with a splash of white wine and let it cook off. Remove from heat and let the sauté cool.

In a bowl, combine cold cooked lentils with the chosen french vegetable (raw endives — strongly preferred, see notes — or raw/shredded turnips or radishes).

Fold in the cooled sautéed leeks (hot-pan version only). Pour the cream vinaigrette over and toss. Fold in chopped fresh parsley (non-optional — see notes). Finish with the chosen nut (slivered almonds, toasted walnuts, or sunflower seeds).
notes:
THE STRUCTURE: cold lentils + cream vinaigrette (cream + mustard + vinegar + garlic) + endives + parsley + nuts. Sautéed leeks are a nice addition when you're building this for others, but not always necessary.

NON-FLEXIBLE (the rules):
- LENTILS COOKED AND COLD (NOT IN THE PAN): the lentils must be cooked, but they stay 'raw' in the sense that they never enter the sauté pan. Adding them to the pan is the trap — once they get mushy and heated, they start demanding stock, tarragon, and other stew-like vegetables, and the raw french vegetable and vinaigrette will clash with that direction. Keep them cold. Combine them with the cooled sauté (if using) and dressing off heat.
- CREAM + MUSTARD + VINEGAR + GARLIC IN THE VINAIGRETTE: all four are primary. See vinaigrette note below.
- ENDIVES: strongly preferred over the other french vegetables — load-bearing (see endives-and-parsley note).
- PARSLEY: non-optional — load-bearing (see endives-and-parsley note).
- NUTS: non-optional. They provide the textural crunch that the cold lentils don't.

=== CREAM VINAIGRETTE — MUSTARD AND VINEGAR BOTH, NOT PICK-ONE ===

The dressing is heavy cream + white wine vinegar + dijon mustard + raw minced garlic. Both the vinegar and the mustard are primary — neither alone is enough. Mustard on its own reads too round-and-savory and loses the sharp french-vinaigrette acid; vinegar on its own reads too sharp without the mustard's emulsifying, depth-adding backbone. Together they lock the dressing into the right register. This is a departure from the previous 'pick one acid' framing — that was wrong; it needs both.

White wine (the actual wine) is now secondary — it's a nice deglaze for the pan version but not what the dressing itself is built on.

=== HOT-PAN LEEKS VS NO-PAN GARLIC-MACERATE — TWO WAYS TO BUILD THIS ===

The sautéed leeks are genuinely nice — softened leek gives the dish a warm allium backbone and reads more 'composed' when you're plating for other people. Reach for the hot-pan version when you're building this for someone else or when you want the fuller expression of the dish.

BUT ONE OF THIS DISH'S ADVANTAGES IS THAT IT CAN BE PREPARED AND SERVED ENTIRELY COLD, WITH NO PAN: everything else in the salad is already cold-format (cooked-and-cooled lentils, raw endives, raw parsley, whisked cream vinaigrette). The leeks are the only thing that would dirty a pan. When making this for myself, I often just skip the leeks entirely and lean harder on raw garlic macerated in the vinaigrette instead. The macerating garlic (a few cloves finely minced, sat in the vinegar + mustard + cream for 10-15 minutes) gives you an allium backbone that stands in for the leeks well enough; the dish reads slightly sharper and less soft, but it's still recognizably itself. The tradeoff is worth it for a weeknight cold salad you can throw together without dirtying a pan.

GARLIC ALWAYS RAW IN THE VINAIGRETTE — either the only allium (no-pan version) or supplementing the sautéed leeks (hot-pan version). It's not optional anymore.

=== ENDIVES + PARSLEY ARE LOAD-BEARING ===

ENDIVES AND PARSLEY ARE THE TWO INGREDIENTS THIS SALAD CAN'T LOSE. The dish's identity — bitter-crunchy-green cold lentil salad in a sharp cream vinaigrette — sits on these two specifically. Endives give you the raw crunchy-bitter body that reads distinctly french and stands up to the cream; parsley gives you the raw green herbaceous backbone that ties the endives to the rest of the salad and keeps the cream from reading flat. Drop either one and the dish stops working:
- WITHOUT ENDIVES (using turnips or radishes instead): the bitterness gets less structural and more diffuse; the salad reads more like a lentil-crunchy-veg bowl than a french salad. Turnips and radishes are backup options for when endives aren't around, but they're a real downgrade.
- WITHOUT PARSLEY: the cream sauce and endives sit alone without a green tie, and the dish reads heavy and one-note. This is why tarragon and mint (both previously tried) got rejected — the herb slot itself is load-bearing; the wrong herb makes it worse, but no herb is even worse than that.
Both together, always. This is more important than any of the acid / nut / garlic details.

NUTS — pick one:
- Slivered almonds (default)
- Toasted walnuts
- Sunflower seeds
All work. Almonds are the canonical choice; the others are variety.

FRENCH VEGETABLE — pick ONE, never more:
- Endives (strongly preferred — see endives-and-parsley note above)
- Turnips (raw or shredded — backup)
- Radishes (raw or shredded — backup)
The 'pick one' constraint is structural — these are all bitter vegetables, and combining two of them makes the dish overwhelmingly bitter (the cream sauce can balance one bitter element, not two).

PARSLEY IS THE HERB — NOT TARRAGON, NOT MINT: chopped fresh parsley folded into the finished salad is the right herb for this dish. Both tarragon (previously listed as 'unnecessary but nice') and mint (previously primary) were wrong on more testing — tarragon pushes the salad toward a stew-lentil / french-cassoulet register the cold-lentil format doesn't want, and mint reads brighter and more middle-eastern than the leek + cream + endive base calls for. Parsley sits cleanly under the cream sauce, adds a green backbone without competing with the leek or endive, and doesn't drag the dish in a different cuisine direction.

=== FAILURE MODE — TOO SOGGY (USUALLY TOO MUCH OLIVE OIL) ===

The main failure mode is a soggy, oil-heavy salad. Usually the culprit is too much olive oil — either during the leek sauté (leeks over-oiled and then that oil ends up in the finished salad) or added later as a finishing drizzle. Olive oil is neither a primary flavor here nor a structural component; it carries much less of the dish's actual flavor than the cream and the vinegar do, and used heavily it just weighs the salad down and coats the lentils and endives in a way that dulls everything else.

Keep the olive oil to the minimum required to sauté the leeks (hot-pan version), and don't add any at the finish. The cream + vinegar + mustard is the fat-and-acid backbone; olive oil doesn't need to supplement it. If the salad is reading soggy on the plate, the fix is almost always less oil next time, not more of something else to compensate.
